quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 11, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Formula Writing Practice
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Writing Chemical Formulas for Ionic Compounds, Covalent Compounds, Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, Stock System

questions.question: What is the formula for aluminum sulfate?
questions.explanation: Aluminum sulfate is $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$. Aluminum has a +3 charge ($\text{Al}^{3+}$), and sulfate is $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ with a -2 charge. To balance charges, two $\text{Al}^{3+}$ (+6) combine with three $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (-6).

questions.question: What is the formula for calcium nitrate?
questions.explanation: Calcium nitrate is $\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2$. Calcium has a +2 charge ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$), and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so two nitrates are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for iron(III) chloride?
questions.explanation: Iron(III) chloride is $\text{FeCl}_3$. Iron(III) has a +3 charge ($\text{Fe}^{3+}$), and chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$ with a -1 charge, so three chlorides are needed. $\text{FeCl}_2$ (A) would be iron(II) chloride.

questions.question: What is the formula for copper(I) oxide?
questions.explanation: Copper(I) oxide is $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$. Copper(I) has a +1 charge ($\text{Cu}^+$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so two copper ions are needed to balance the charge.

questions.question: What is the formula for potassium sulfate?
questions.explanation: Potassium sulfate is $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$. Potassium has a +1 charge ($\text{K}^+$), and sulfate is $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so two potassium ions are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for ammonium phosphate?
questions.explanation: Ammonium phosphate is $(\text{NH}_4)_3\text{PO}_4$. Ammonium has a +1 charge ($\text{NH}_4^+$), and phosphate is $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ with a -3 charge, so three ammonium ions are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for barium hydroxide?
questions.explanation: Barium hydroxide is $\text{Ba(OH)}_2$. Barium has a +2 charge ($\text{Ba}^{2+}$), and hydroxide is $\text{OH}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two hydroxide ions are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for tin(IV) chloride?
questions.explanation: Tin(IV) chloride is $\text{SnCl}_4$. Tin(IV) has a +4 charge ($\text{Sn}^{4+}$), and chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$ with a -1 charge, so four chlorides are needed. $\text{SnCl}_2$ (A) would be tin(II) chloride.

questions.question: What is the formula for lead(II) sulfide?
questions.explanation: Lead(II) sulfide is $\text{PbS}$. Lead(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Pb}^{2+}$), and sulfide is $\text{S}^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for zinc nitrate?
questions.explanation: Zinc nitrate is $\text{Zn(NO}_3)_2$. Zinc typically forms $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so two nitrates are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for hydrogen peroxide?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide is $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$. It consists of two hydrogen atoms and two oxygen atoms, with an oxygen-oxygen single bond. It is a common bleaching agent and disinfectant.

questions.question: What is the formula for nitrogen trichloride?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen trichloride is $\text{NCl}_3$. It is a covalent compound where nitrogen forms three single bonds with three chlorine atoms. The prefix 'tri-' indicates three chlorine atoms.

questions.question: What is the formula for calcium phosphate?
questions.explanation: Calcium phosphate is $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$. Calcium has a +2 charge ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$), and phosphate is $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ with a -3 charge. To balance charges, three $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ (+6) combine with two $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ (-6).

questions.question: What is the formula for phosphorus pentachloride?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus pentachloride is $\text{PCl}_5$. Phosphorus forms five single bonds with five chlorine atoms, utilizing an expanded octet. The prefix 'penta-' indicates five chlorine atoms.

questions.question: What is the formula for potassium carbonate?
questions.explanation: Potassium carbonate is $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$. Potassium has a +1 charge ($\text{K}^+$), and carbonate is $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so two potassium ions are needed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered writing chemical formulas! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review charge balancing for compounds with polyatomic ions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on determining the correct charges for ions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review how to write formulas for ionic and covalent compounds, including those with transition metals and polyatomic ions.
